title: Annual Report on Open Market Operations - FEDERAL RESERVE BANK of NEW YORK

source.name: New York Fed Markets Data Hub

summary: The Federal Reserve's SOMA portfolio decreased by nearly $700 billion during 2024, driven by balance sheet runoff under FOMC-directed monthly caps. The FOMC slowed the pace of runoff in June by reducing the Treasury redemption cap from $60 billion to $25 billion per month. Treasury holdings fell from $4.79 trillion to $4.29 trillion while agency MBS holdings declined from $2.42 trillion to $2.23 trillion.
key_points: SOMA holdings of Treasuries and agency MBS decreased by approximately $700 billion during 2024
FOMC reduced Treasury securities redemption cap from $60 billion to $25 billion monthly in June 2024
Treasury securities holdings declined from $4.79 trillion to $4.29 trillion, representing roughly 70% of total SOMA runoff
Agency MBS holdings decreased from $2.42 trillion to $2.23 trillion, representing roughly 30% of total SOMA runoff
SOMA securities comprise 95% of total Federal Reserve assets
Agency MBS monthly cap remained at $35 billion with no reinvestments as principal payments stayed below cap
why_it_matters: The pace and structure of Federal Reserve balance sheet reduction directly influences bank reserve levels, money market conditions, and the supply of securities in the financial system. The June 2024 adjustment of the Treasury cap signals the FOMC's willingness to moderate QT pace in response to market conditions.
market_structure_context: The FOMC uses monthly caps to control the pace of balance sheet runoff, creating a predictable framework for securities redemption. Treasury and MBS markets are affected by the changing composition and absolute size of the Federal Reserve's securities holdings.
notes: Published_at date is empty in source metadata. The content provides quantitative specifics about Fed balance sheet runoff but does not discuss market impact, price reactions, or broader macro implications.
